title: Aas,Indore vs Exemption Ward Indore, Indore on 21 May, 2026
body: Aas,Indore vs Exemption Ward Indore, Indore on 21 May, 2026 आयकर अपीलीय अिधकरण, इंदौर ायपीठ, इंदौर IN THE INCOME TAX APPELLATE TRIBUNAL INDORE BENCH, INDORE BEFORE SHRI B.M. BIYANI, ACCOUNTANT MEMBER AND SHRI PARESH M JOSHI, JUDICIAL MEMBER ITA No.316/Ind/2025 (AY: 2022-23) AAS, बनाम/ EXEMPTION 1303,HARE KRISHNA VIHAR, Vs. WARD INDORE NEAR RAJBAG GARDEN, NIPANIYA, Indore (PAN:AACTA5032C) (Appellant) (Respondent) Assessee by Sh. Anand Prabhawalkar, Adv. Revenue by Shri Ashish Porwal, Sr. DR Date of Hearing 11.05.2026 Date of Pronouncement 21.05.2026 आदेश / O R D E R Per Paresh M Joshi, J.M.: This is an Appeal filed by the Assessee under section 253 of the income tax Act 1961, [hereinafter referred to as the Act for the sake of brevity] before this tribunal as and by way of a second appeal. The Assessee is aggrieved by the order bearing No:-ITBA/APL/S/250/2024-25/1072778289(1) dated 31.01.2025 passed by the Ld. CIT (A) u/s 250 of the Act, which is hereinafter referred to as the "Impugned Order". The Relevant Assessment year is 2022-23 and the Page 1 of 12 AAS ITA No. 316/Ind/2025 - A.Y.2022-23 corresponding previous year period is from 01.04.2021 to 31.03.2022. 2. Factual Matrix 2.1 That as and by way of an "Intimation Order" made u/s 143(1) of the act, the total Income of the assesse was computed and assessed at Rs. 1,38,49,370/- against the ROI of Rs. 2,35,532/-. Consequently demand of Rs. 57, 96,150/- was created vide order no: - CPC /2223/A7/326953491, Receipt no: - 875603800281222 Demand no: - 2023202237155975293T dated 02.06.2023 which is hereinafter referred to as "Impugned Intimation Order". 2.2 That the assesse is registered u/s 12AA of the Act. 2.3 That the assesse had filed an original ROI on 07.11.2022 and the revised ROI was filed on 28.12.2022. That while filing the revised ROI the auditor failed to file the audit report in the form 10B along with ROI itself but the same was however filed on 21.01.2023. 2.4 That the assessee during the year under consideration had received total receipt of Rs. 1, 38, 49,370/- against Page 2 of 12 AAS ITA No. 316/Ind/2025 - A.Y.2022-23 which the assessee claimed application of Income of Rs. 1,36,13,838/- resulting in balance of Rs. 2,35,532/- being taxable total Income. The Ld. CPC while processing the return u/s 143(1) determined the taxable total Income at Rs. 1,38,49,370/-. The benefit of the application of aforesaid Income was not given due to non-filing of audit report within the prescribed time limit. 2.5 That the assessee being aggrieved by the aforesaid "Impugned Intimation Order" prefers the first appeal u/s 246A of the act before the Ld. CIT (A) who by the "Impugned Order" has dismissed the first appeal of the assessee on the grounds and reasons stated therein. The core grounds and reasons for the dismissal of the first appeal was as under: "6. Observation of this office: - 6.1. The statement of facts, grounds of appeal, and the order appealed against, written submissions, and copies of documentary evidence/explanations have been thoroughly examined. The central issue for adjudication among grounds of appeal no. 1 to 4 raised pertains to the sole issue of denying the benefit of Section 11 & Section 12 of the Act while processing the return of Income under section 143(1) of the Act as Form 10B was not filed within the prescribed time limit. Therefore, all these grounds of appeal are being clubbed and adjudicated together. 6.2 It is noted that while processing the return of Income, the AO denied the exemption claimed u/s 11 and 12 . Page 3 of 12 AAS ITA No. 316/Ind/2025 - A.Y.2022-23 6.3 CBDT has issued circular No.16/2022 dated 19/07/2022 issued by Ministry of Finance authorizing the Commissioners of Income tax to admit applications of condonation of delay in filing form No. 10B for AY 2018-19 or for any Subsequent Assessment Years where there is delay of upto 365 days and decide on merits. 6.4 In this context, it's important to note that according to Section 12A(1)(b) and Rule 17B, Trusts are obligated to file Form 108, which is mandatory. Form 108 can only be accessed and submitted online and must be filed no later than the specified date mentioned in Section 44AB of the Act, which is one month before the due date for submitting the Income Tax Return under Section 139(1) of the Act. 6.5 The appellant in its written submission has stated that the original Return was filed on 07/11/2022 and the revised return was filed on 28/12/2022 and while filing the revised return the learned auditor failed to file the Audit Report along with the return itself but the same was filed on 21/01/2023. However, from the intimation u/s 143(1) of the Act dated 02/06/2023 it is noticed that the original ITR was filed only on 28/12/2022 as against the due date 07/11/2022. Thus, these facts stated by the appellant are found incorrect. Further, the appellant submitted Form 108 on 21/01/2023, while the deadline for filing Form 10B was one month prior to the due date for submitting the Return. The extended due date for submitting the return of income in the case of the appellant was 07/11/2022, hence, Form 10B was not filed within the time frame outlined by the Act. 6.6 In view of the above, I find no error in the AO's decision to disallow the appellant's claim for exemption under Section 11 of the Act while processing the return under Section 143(1) of the Act. 6.7 Further, Section 119 empowers the Board to issue orders and guidelines regarding the condonation of delay in compliance with any provision of the Act. Section 119(2) reads as follows: Section 119(2) (a).... (b) the Board may, if it considers it desirable or expedient so to do for avoiding genuine hardship in any case or class of cases, by general or special order, authorise any income-tax authority, not being a Joint Commissioner (Appeals) or a Commissioner (Appeals) to admit an application or claim for any exemption, deduction, Page 4 of 12 AAS ITA No. 316/Ind/2025 - A.Y.2022-23 refund or any other relief under this Act after the expiry of the period specified by or under this Act for making such application or claim and deal with the same on merits in accordance with law." 6.8 In this context, the Central Board of Direct Taxes (CBDT) issued Circular 3/2020 dated 03-01-2020 and Circular No. 16/2022 dated 19th July, 2022. The relevant excerpts from Circular No. 16/2022 dated 19th July, 2022 are as follows: "Condonation of delay under Section 119(2)(b) of the Income- tax Act, 1961 in filing of Form No. 10B for Assessment Year 2018-19 and subsequent years -Reg. In exercise of the powers conferred under section 119(2) of the Income-tax Act, 1961 (hereinafter referred to as 'Act'), the Central Board of Direct Taxes (CBDT) by Circular No.2/2020 (F.No. 197/55/2018-ITA-1] dated 03.01.2020 authorized the Commissioners of Income-tax to admit applications of condonation of delay in filing Form No. 10B for AY 2018-19 or for any subsequent Assessment Years where there is delay of upto 365 days and decide on merits. 2. Further to the powers delegated to Commissioners of Income- tax as discussed above, the CBDT hereby directs that where there is delay of beyond 365 days upto three years in filing Form No. 10B for Assessment Year 2018-19 or for any subsequent Assessment Years, the Pr. Chief Commissioners of Income-tax / Chief Commissioners of Income-tax are authorized to admit such applications of condonation of delay under section 119(2) of the Act and decide on merits. 3. The Pr. Chief Commissioner/Chief Commissioner or Commissioners of Income-tax, as the case may be, while entertaining such applications for condonation of delay in filing Form No. 108, shall satisfy themselves that the applicant was prevented by reasonable cause from filing such Form within the stipulated time. 4. Further, the Pr. Chief Commissioner/ Chief Commissioner of Income-tax, as the case may be, shall preferably dispose the application within three months of receipt of the application." 6.9. It is evident from the CBDT Circulars and Section 119(2)(b) that the Assessing Officer(AO), Joint Commissioner of Income Tax (Appeals) [JCIT(A)], and Commissioner of Income Tax (Appeals) Page 5 of 12 AAS ITA No. 316/Ind/2025 - A.Y.2022-23 [CIT(A)] do not have the authority under Section 119(2)(b) of the Act to grant condonation for the delay in filing Form 10B. Therefore, I find no fault in the AO's decision to not consider the appellant's condonation request during the return processing. Neither the AO during return processing nor I, the JCIT(Appeals) when finalizing the appeal, are empowered by the statute to entertain the appellant's condonation application for Form 10B. The appellant has the option to approach the jurisdictional Commissioner of Income Tax, Chief Commissioner of Income Tax, or Principal Chief Commissioner of Income Tax for the condonation of the delay in filing Form 10B and to claim the benefits of Sections 11 and 12 of the Act. Hence, I find no merit in the Grounds no. 1 to 4 of the current appeal and, as a result, Grounds no. 1 to 4 of the appeal are dismissed. 6.10. Ground no. 5 of the appeal relates to the appellant's contention that additions /disallowance on a mere ground that details at Sr. No. A19 of Schedule Part- A General are not matching with the information as per Form 10 AC is purely a hyper technical approach and cannot be sustained in the eye of law. With regard to this ground of appeal the appellant has not made submissions. Thus, it is assumed that this ground of appeal is not pressed. Moreover, it is observed that the exemptions claimed u/s 11 of the Act have been disallowed by the AO, CPC mainly on the ground that Form no. 10B has not been filed within stipulated time. Thus, this ground of appeal is treated dismissed for statistical purposes. 6.11. Ground no. 6 of the appeal is general in nature and do not require adjudication. 6. Decision: - In the result, the appellant's appeal is Dismissed. " 2.6 That the assessee being aggrieved by the "Impugned Order" has preferred the instant second appeal before this tribunal and has raised the following grounds of appeal in Page 6 of 12 AAS ITA No. 316/Ind/2025 - A.Y.2022-23 the form no. 36 against the "Impugned Order" which are as under:- "1.Because, the impugned order dated 31-01-2025 passed by the learned Additional/Joint Commissioner of Income Tax is based upon perverse findings of facts and thus deserves to be set aside quashed on this ground alone. The finding regarding the return under consideration being an Original Return and not the Revised return as claimed by the appellant is an incorrect factual finding based upon total non-application of mind by the appellate authority to the facts of the case. 2.Because, the impugned order dated 31-01-2025 passed by the learned Additional/Joint Commissioner of Income Tax is also against the principles of natural justice and as such deserves to 2 be quashed/ set aside on this ground alone. The appellate authority has failed to appreciate that in the Written submission filed by the appellant, the appellant had supported its contentions by citing various judicial pronouncements which have not been discussed in the order at all. 3.Because, the impugned order dated 31-01-2025 passed by the learned Additional / Joint Commissioner of Income Tax is an unreasoned and a cryptic order where he has decided the grounds number 1 to 4 by writing certain wrong facts and giving conclusion in one line "in view of above, I find no error in the AO's decision". Thus, according to the applicant the impugned order suffers from a serious error apparent on record that the appellate authority has not considered the written submission on merits at all and has merely decided the appeal on assumption of certain wrong facts and hence deserves to be quashed/set aside. 4.Because the authorities below have erred in law in assessing the taxable income at Rs 1,38,49,370/- of the appellant and not 4 considering the application of such income by the appellant only on the ground of delayed submission of Audit Report in Form 10B. 5.Because the authorities below have failed to consider that the delay in submission of audit report in prescribed Form No 10B is 5 a technical/venial breach/default which cannot lead to make the receipts taxable which otherwise is not includable in the income of the appellant. 6.Because, the authorities below have also failed to consider that default of delay in submission of audit report in Form 10B is not a fatal default/breach leading to taxability of the receipt particularly when the said audit report was otherwise ready and signed on 07/11/2022 and it was merely uploaded on 21/01//2023. Page 7 of 12 AAS ITA No. 316/Ind/2025 - A.Y.2022-23 7.Because the authorities below failed to appreciate that the delay in uploading the audit report in form 10B cannot be attributable to the appellant as the audit was completed within prescribed time and the audit report was also signed by the Chartered Accountant before the time prescribed, only uploading of the same was not done within the time prescribed. 8. Because the authorities below failed to appreciate that additions disallowance on a mere ground that 'details at Sr. No A19 of 8 Schedule Part-A General are not matching with the information as per Form 10 AC' is purely a hypertechnical approach and cannot be sustained in the eye of law. 9.That appellant craves leave of this authority to add, amend or alter the grounds as and when necessary." 3. Record of Hearing 3.1 The hearing in the matter took place before this Tribunal on 11.05.2026 when the Ld. AR for & on behalf of the Assessee appeared before us & interalia contended that the "Impugned Order" is bad in law, illegal and not proper. It is in the violation of the principle of natural justice. It therefore deserves to be set aside. It was next submitted that the original ROI was filed on 07.11.2022 PB pages 2 to 48. The revised ROI was filed on 28.12.2022 pages 49 to 95 of PB. The Acknowledgement of e-filing of form 10B on 21.01.2023 are at pages 96 to 100 of PB. Audit report in physical form is dated 07.11.2022 along with UDIN from pages 101 to 111 of PB. Impugned "Intimation Order" is dated 02.06.2023 whereas 10B was filed on 21.01.2023 Page 8 of 12 AAS ITA No. 316/Ind/2025 - A.Y.2022-23 which ought to have been seen by the Ld. CPC . It was submitted that in view of above facts the lower authorities have erred in law as the benefit of form no. 10B is not given and the demand created is illegal and same should be set aside. Per contra the Ld. DR submitted that he relies upon the orders of the lower authorities. Hearing was over and closed. 4. Observations Findings & conclusions 4.1 We have to decide the legality, validity and proprietary of the "impugned order" basis records of the case & the rival submission canvassed before us. 4.2 We have carefully perused the records of the case and have heard the submissions. 4.3 We basis records of the case & after hearing & further upon examining the rival contentions of the Ld. AR and the Ld. DR canvassed before us, are of the considered opinion that the "Impugned Intimation Order" which was sustained by the "Impugned Order" is bad in law, and not proper as we are of the considered opinion that substantial Page 9 of 12 AAS ITA No. 316/Ind/2025 - A.Y.2022-23 benefit of form 10B and so also the application of income to the extent of Rs. 1,36,13,386/- should not be denied to the assessee for a failure to upload the same within time stipulated by law. Further basis PB we notice that form no. 10B was uploaded finally on 21.01.2013 and audit report in physical form was signed on 07.11.2022. This issue too is squarely covered by recent decision of this bench in ITA No. - 17&18/Ind/2026 dated 08.05.2026 [Smt. Chouthi Bai Agarwal v/s ITO (E) Indore wherein the Para 8 following was held as under: "8. The ITAT, Indore has consistently accepted in numerous decisions that the requirement of filing Audit Report [Form No. 10B] in time is one of the conditions for claiming the benefit of exemption u/s 11 / 12 but it is a procedural-cum-directory requirement and even if the report is not filed in time but subsequently filed to AO, the exemption u/s 11 / 12 can't be denied. A few decisions of ITAT, Indore are cited below: (a) ITAT, Indore - Indore Contract Bridge Association Vs. CPC , Bangalore in ITA No. 403/Ind/2022 order dated 18.04.2023 (b) ITAT, Indore - Navratna Sukrat Foundation Vs. CPC , Bangalore, ITA No. 390/Ind/2022 dated 21.04.2023 (c) ITAT, Indore - DCIT Vs. Shri Vaishnav Polytechnic College, Indore ITA No. 469/Ind/2018 order dated 06.11.2020" 4.4 Therefore, in light of above facts and duly following our own decision (Supra) we are of the considered opinion that Page 10 of 12 AAS ITA No. 316/Ind/2025 - A.Y.2022-23 the assessee cannot be denied the benefit of application of Income u/s 11 / 12 as claimed in the ROI for mere delay in filing of audit report [form 10B] when ultimately such report stands filed before "Impugned Intimation Order" dated 02.06.2023. 4.5 In premises set out herein above the Impugned Order is set aside and we however restore the issue to the file of the Ld. AO for the limited purpose of re-computing the income and allowing the exemption in accordance with law, after accepting the audit report. Form 10B filed by the assessee (supra). Assessee succeeds in appeal. Ordered accordingly. 4.6 In the result appeal of the assessee is allowed for statistical purpose. Pronounced in open court on 21.05.2026. Sd/- Sd/- (BHAGIRATH MAL BIYANI) (PARESH M JOSHI) ACCOUNTANT MEMBER JUDICIAL MEMBER Indore Dated : 21/05/2026 Patel/Sr. PS Page 11 of 12 AAS ITA No. 316/Ind/2025 - A.Y.2022-23 Copies to: (1) The appellant (2) The respondent (3) CIT (4) CIT(A) (5) Departmental Representative (6) Guard File By order UE COPY Senior Private Secretary Income Tax Appellate Tribunal Indore Bench, Indore Page 12 of 12
